name: Skyvern
maintenance_status: Actively maintained. The public repository showed release v1.0.46 on 2026-07-08, after many material 2025-2026 releases covering adaptive caching, browser profiles, CDP browser control, OpenTelemetry, authentication, security hardening, and workflow reliability.
license: The core repository is AGPL-3.0, matching ApplyPilot's AGPL-3.0 license family. Modified or network-deployed derivative code must preserve AGPL source-availability obligations. A separately deployed Skyvern service with an API boundary is operationally cleaner, and managed-cloud-only anti-bot features are not part of the open-source grant.
pricing_model: [uncertain] Current official material is internally inconsistent. The January 30, 2026 pricing announcement lists Free at $0 for about 1,000 credits or 170 actions, Hobby at $29/month for about 30,000 credits or 1,200 actions, Pro at $149/month for about 150,000 credits or 6,200 actions, and Enterprise as custom. It says credit use varies with runtime, page complexity, retries, and anti-bot measures. Other current official pages still describe per-step billing or advertise a different free-credit allowance, so production pricing must be confirmed in the account billing UI or an order form.
marginal_cost_per_attempt: [uncertain] Managed-cloud cash marginal cost is zero while an already-paid monthly credit allowance remains, but the economic unit cost at full plan utilization is about $0.024 per listed action on either Hobby ($29/1,200) or Pro ($149/6,200). An illustrative 12-30-action long-tail application therefore consumes about $0.29-$0.72 of plan value before retries, persistent-session billing, or human recovery. Skyvern does not publish a job-application action distribution, and credits are not a fixed one-action unit.
projected_cost_per_verified_apply: [uncertain] No ATS-stratified verified completion rate exists. For a Skyvern-only long-tail route, use (vendor value per attempt + human recovery + amortized integration maintenance) / positively verified completion rate. Using only the illustrative $0.29-$0.72 attempt value and an unproven 50%-80% verified rate gives $0.36-$1.44 before human and maintenance cost. It beats ApplyPilot's later $1.8530 all-in baseline only if completion, credit utilization, and duplicate-safe confirmation are strong; this is a benchmark hypothesis, not a forecast.
billing_granularity: [uncertain] Monthly plans bundle credits and concurrency. Credit burn varies with runtime, page complexity, retries, proxy or anti-bot work, and the current changelog also references separate persistent-browser-session uptime metering. Overage price, unused-credit rollover, minimum charge per run, idle-session rules, storage retention charges, and Enterprise egress terms are not stated consistently in public current material.
model_cost: [uncertain] Managed plans bundle Skyvern-selected model execution into credits rather than exposing token invoices. Self-hosting supports bring-your-own OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, or Ollama models, so token cost depends on engine and page/action count. `max_steps`, economy-tree mode, cached scripts, and later no-model replay can reduce calls, but no official job-application token profile is published.
infrastructure_cost: [uncertain] Cloud browser, proxy, model, artifact, and control-plane infrastructure is bundled in managed credits. Self-hosting requires the Skyvern application stack, Chromium, database and queue services, artifact storage, and optional model endpoints; no official per-attempt CPU, RAM, storage, or power benchmark is published. ApplyPilot should measure these costs rather than assume self-hosting is free.
human_recovery_cost: [uncertain] Hobby includes stored credentials, Pro includes TOTP and 1Password, and Enterprise advertises human-in-the-loop workflows. These can reduce login work, but there is no published job-application rate for stale profiles, OTP waits, ambiguous confirmation, or owner takeover. Budget human minutes separately for every pause, profile refresh, and `completed` run lacking positive ATS evidence.
adapter_maintenance_tco: [uncertain] Initial integration is materially larger than adding a locator: route orchestration, workflow definitions, parameter and answer mapping, cloud or self-host operations, artifact joins, cost reconciliation, and submit quarantine are required. Adaptive script caching may reduce repeated locator maintenance, but cached scripts introduce validation and invalidation work. No ApplyPilot engineering-hours-per-1,000-applies estimate is available.
verified_completion_rate: [uncertain] Unknown. Skyvern publishes a job-application workflow cookbook and general form-filling claims, but the cookbook uses a demo portal and treats a completed workflow as success; it is not a matched Ashby, Greenhouse, Lever, Workable, Workday, and long-tail benchmark with positive submission evidence.
failure_modes: Important failure classes are stale or expired postings, policy denial, wrong element or option selection, incomplete required questions, unsupported custom widgets, fabricated or weak free text, login or OTP waits, CAPTCHA or challenge states, stale/corrupt profiles, browser or CDP tunnel loss, model timeout, cached-script drift, workflow retry duplication, and `completed` status without a confirmed submit. Skyvern's release history also documents fixes for iframe trees, dropdowns, profile corruption, final-status races, browser timeouts, and script-cache regressions, which should become canary fixtures.
crash_recovery: [uncertain] Runs have durable IDs, statuses, failure reasons, screenshots, video, HAR, structured logs, webhooks, step counts, termination, retries, and workflow-level continue/finally controls. Browser profiles and reset APIs help recover state, and cached scripts support replay. Public docs do not promise step-level transactional resume after a browser or actor crash, so ApplyPilot must classify pre-submit versus post-submit failure and quarantine ambiguous runs instead of blindly rerunning.
exactly_once_submit_safety: [uncertain] Skyvern has workflow blocks, validation, run IDs, and status controls but no documented native idempotency key, duplicate-application lookup, or exactly-once submit protocol. ApplyPilot must own a durable pre-submit checkpoint, one-attempt lease, submit-intent record, post-click network/DOM evidence, and `crash_unconfirmed` quarantine before any retry.
verification_evidence_tier: [uncertain] Native evidence includes action logs with reasoning, screenshots after actions, end-to-end video, HAR/network artifacts, run output, downloaded files, and failure reasons. A workflow can add extraction or validation after submit, but the documented job-application recipe only prints success when the workflow status is `completed`. For ApplyPilot, accept ATS application ID or success response first, known confirmation DOM/URL second, confirmation email third, and never a generic completed status alone.
verification_latency: [uncertain] Runs can be polled synchronously or reported by webhook; browser evidence is available during or immediately after execution, while confirmation email remains delayed. No p50/p95 run or reconciliation latency is published for job applications. Measure submit-to-DOM confirmation and submit-to-email confirmation separately.
headless_headed_parity: [uncertain] Skyvern uses Chromium and can control cloud, self-hosted, local headed, or local headless browsers. Local browser serving can reuse a Chrome profile and expose a headless flag. No ATS-stratified headed-versus-headless parity data is published, and authentication, extension, and challenge behavior may differ.
python_integration: Strong. The current SDK supports Python 3.11-3.13, compatible with ApplyPilot's Python >=3.11 runtime, and exposes Python workflow APIs plus AI-enhanced Playwright page methods such as `act`, `extract`, and `validate`. A REST API and webhooks are also available if Skyvern is isolated as a service.
playwright_cdp_reuse: Moderate to strong. The SDK layers AI methods on Playwright, cloud sessions expose a Playwright page over CDP, self-hosted documentation references CDP connect, and `skyvern browser serve` can launch Chrome with an existing profile directory, cookies, extensions, and passwords. Lifecycle and port ownership still need an adapter so Skyvern does not conflict with ApplyPilot's current Chrome/CDP process.
migration_effort: [uncertain] Large for a submit-owning route, medium for shadow-only long-tail experiments. A safe integration needs a Skyvern client/service, workflow templates, ApplyPilot profile and answer parameters, explicit submit separation, evidence ingestion, cost accounting, failure mapping, canary policy, and cloud/self-host security review. Replacing the current agent wholesale would have a larger blast radius than invoking Skyvern only after deterministic and semantic routes fail.
ats_fit: [uncertain] Promising for variable long-tail hosts because Skyvern combines DOM, screenshots, LLM decisions, file upload, authentication, loops, conditionals, and validation. It publishes a job-application pipeline and lists Workday integration, but there is no positive-evidence benchmark for Ashby, Greenhouse, Lever, Workable, Workday tenants, or real long-tail hosts. Stable ATS flows remain better candidates for deterministic adapters.
deterministic_coverage: [uncertain] First encounters are model-assisted, not deterministic. Successful repeated workflows may generate cached scripts and replay portions without model calls, but cache eligibility, warm hit rate, and job-form invalidation rate are unknown. Deterministic adapters should continue to own known Ashby/Greenhouse variants; Skyvern should target the residual long tail.
escape_rate: [uncertain] Unknown. Escapes include unsupported required questions, sensitive questions, stale auth, challenges, uncertain submit state, low-confidence validation, and cached-script mismatch. Measure separate semantic-agent, premium-agent, and human escape rates rather than combining them into one failure total.
control_plane_overhead: [uncertain] Higher than a semantic-locator layer because an action step can involve screenshot and DOM capture, model decision, browser action, artifact storage, and API or webhook round trips. Workflow blocks reduce planning scope, `use_economy_tree` reduces DOM context, and adaptive caching can eliminate later AI turns, but no bytes, tokens, or p95 round-trip profile is published for application forms.
observability_joinability: Strong if integrated deliberately. Skyvern exposes run and browser-session IDs, statuses, failure reasons, action timelines, recordings, screenshots, HAR files, downloaded-file checksums, step counts, webhooks, and OpenTelemetry in current releases. ApplyPilot must persist its attempt ID as a workflow parameter/tag and map every Skyvern run to `apply_result_events`, cost rows, browser artifacts, and later email evidence.
persistent_profiles: Strong. Browser sessions preserve live state for 5 minutes to 24 hours, and browser profiles archive cookies, localStorage, session files, and auth tokens for later reuse. Profile creation is asynchronous, tokens can expire, profiles can corrupt, and current docs recommend periodic refresh/testing and provide reset/delete paths. Profile locking and one-account concurrency remain ApplyPilot responsibilities.
existing_session_attach: Supported with boundaries. `skyvern browser serve` launches or exposes local Chrome through a secured connection and can point at a profile directory; self-hosted CDP connect is documented. The local tunnel must use API-key authentication and preferably VPN/mTLS/IP controls because access grants full browser control. Direct attachment to an arbitrary already-owned ApplyPilot tab needs a proof-of-concept.
login_and_otp: Strong feature coverage: stored credentials, dedicated login actions, password-manager or vault backends, TOTP, email/SMS 2FA flows, notifications, webhooks, and human interaction are documented. Pro is the first public plan listing 2FA/TOTP; Enterprise advertises human-in-the-loop. ApplyPilot should still route owner decisions and account recovery to its supervised auth lane.
extension_support: Local browser serving explicitly reuses installed extensions. Skyvern does not document a per-workflow extension permission allowlist or native-messaging policy. Use a dedicated cloned profile with only approved extensions and never expose a general personal profile through an unauthenticated tunnel.
session_data_boundary: Managed Cloud may process and retain prompts, URLs, page content, screenshots, files, workflow definitions, run history, browser/session state, and connected credentials as needed for the service. Credential docs state secrets are fetched just in time, replaced with placeholders for LLMs, masked in artifacts, and can remain in external vaults. Self-hosting keeps browser and application data in ApplyPilot-controlled infrastructure, but any configured external model still receives non-secret page context; telemetry is enabled by default in the open-source repo unless disabled.
required_field_completeness: [uncertain] Workflows support extraction schemas, validation blocks, and granular action blocks, and the job-application recipe extracts questions before generating answers. There is no documented guarantee that all visible, hidden, conditional, or client-validated required fields are enumerated before submit. ApplyPilot must independently enumerate required controls, compare them with the answer plan, and refuse when any required mapping is absent.
answer_provenance: [uncertain] Skyvern can accept resume/profile data as workflow parameters, but its generic text-prompt and browser-agent blocks do not provide ApplyPilot's evidence-only answer policy by default. Keep answer generation in ApplyPilot's verifier-gated answerer and pass approved values into Skyvern; do not let a freeform workflow infer employers, dates, metrics, legal status, salary, disability, demographic, or sponsorship answers.
unsupported_question_fallback: [uncertain] Workflows can branch, validate, continue on failure, call custom code, and use Human Interaction, but safe job-question escalation is not an out-of-box contract. Define an explicit `unsupported_question` output that halts before submit and routes to approved-answer lookup, premium agent, or owner review. Never treat a blank value or guessed answer as a recoverable browser error.
prompt_injection_resistance: [uncertain] Current releases include Workflow Copilot prompt-injection hardening, code-block sandbox fixes, secret placeholder isolation, and SSRF controls. These are positive signals, not a complete hostile-page guarantee for an acting browser model. ApplyPilot still needs domain allowlists, navigation restrictions, blocked file and network schemes, secret scoping, fixed workflow instructions, adversarial-page tests, and a deterministic submit gate.
site_permission_and_terms: [uncertain] Skyvern's MCP documentation says to connect only accounts, credentials, and websites the operator is authorized to automate. No public source reviewed establishes permission from each ATS or employer for automated job submission. ApplyPilot must apply employer/ATS terms, robots and rate policies where relevant, avoid access-control or CAPTCHA bypass recommendations, and keep challenge pages in supervised or denied lanes.
irreversible_action_guard: [uncertain] Workflow design can place extraction and validation before a separate granular Action/Navigation submit block, which is suitable for a two-phase plan. The platform does not document an unbypassable final-submit consent boundary. ApplyPilot must retain the actual guard: complete verified plan, policy approval, durable submit intent, one authorized click, and positive confirmation or ambiguity quarantine.
concurrency: Managed public plans list 1 concurrent run on Free, 10 on Hobby, 25 on Pro, and custom/unlimited on Enterprise. Self-hosted concurrency is limited by browser, model, database, queue, and worker resources. ApplyPilot should also enforce per-account and per-profile concurrency of one where an ATS session can be invalidated or duplicated.
startup_latency: [uncertain] Cloud runs allocate a browser and self-hosted runs launch or attach Chromium; warm sessions avoid repeated login and launch. Skyvern publishes no p50/p95 cold browser, workflow start, or first-action latency for current plans. Record queue, allocation, navigation, first-model, and first-action timestamps in canaries.
warm_session_reuse: Strong. Live browser sessions preserve tabs, cookies, storage, and page context for chained operations, profiles restore archived authentication state across runs, local browser serve reuses a profile directory, and adaptive caching can reuse generated scripts. Use bounded lifetimes, health checks, and profile locks to avoid stale or cross-attempt state.
tracing_and_replay: Strong tracing, partial replay. Native artifacts include action and reasoning logs, screenshots, video, HAR, structured run data, downloaded-file checksums, and OpenTelemetry support. Adaptive cached scripts can replay learned actions, but deterministic time-travel replay of arbitrary model runs is not promised. Retain Playwright/HAR evidence around submit and version every workflow/cache key.
deployment_modes: Managed cloud, Python or TypeScript SDK against cloud, REST/MCP, Docker Compose self-hosting, on-premises cloud infrastructure, and a hybrid local-browser tunnel are documented. The safest ApplyPilot experiment is an isolated self-host or dedicated cloud workspace with a narrow client boundary and separate browser profile.
browser_version_matrix: Skyvern documentation consistently describes real Chromium/Chrome plus CDP and Playwright. Local Windows, macOS, and Linux Chrome paths are documented. Firefox, WebKit, and WebDriver BiDi support are not documented, so treat this as Chromium-only for planning.
canary_stop_loss: [uncertain] Available primitives include `max_steps`, run and browser-session timeouts, task termination, workflow validation, concurrency plan limits, schedules, error mappings, and webhooks. A native per-route dollar cap, duplicate-submit breaker, or rolling verified-failure circuit breaker is not documented. ApplyPilot must enforce attempt count, spend, time, post-submit ambiguity, and failure-rate stop losses externally.
benchmark_design: Run Skyvern only after deterministic adapters and the AgentQL-style semantic route decline. Use at least 300 shadow attempts stratified across Ashby, Greenhouse, Lever, Workable, Workday tenant, and long-tail hosts, with the same jobs mirrored to the current agent where safe. Then canary no more than 20 submit-authorized attempts per host class. Record required-field recall, answer provenance, actions, credits, wall time, fallback, human minutes, confirmation tier, duplicates, and positive verified submits. Keep challenge and login-wall cases supervised.
route_funnel: For every eligible job record: preflight disposition -> deterministic adapter eligibility -> semantic-locator eligibility -> Skyvern shadow selection -> workflow started -> form discovered -> required plan complete -> auth/challenge state -> submit authorized -> click observed -> synchronous confirmation -> delayed email reconciliation -> verified applied, failed, blocked, or crash_unconfirmed. Preserve both selected route and every fallback reason.
step_context_budget: [uncertain] Canary budget: no more than 30 AI/action steps, one browser session, one profile, one resume upload, and a hard wall-clock limit per attempt; record actual step_count, screenshots, DOM context mode, model/engine, cache status, credits, and output size. The 30-step ceiling is an experimental guard, not a measured optimum.
cache_replay_yield: [uncertain] Skyvern supports adaptive script caching and no-model replay for repeated workflows, including control flow in recent releases. No public ATS warm-hit, invalidation, or successful no-model replay rate exists. Measure per host and workflow version: cache eligible, cache used, fallback-to-AI, stale-cache refusal, and verified completion; never reuse a cache across materially different tenant forms without validation.
confidence_intervals: [uncertain] No vendor or ApplyPilot interval can be computed before a matched canary. Report Wilson 95% intervals for verified completion, required-field miss, duplicate/ambiguous submit, and fallback rates; bootstrap attempts for cost per verified apply because cost is skewed. Do not promote while the upper confidence bound for duplicate or unverified submit exceeds the current route.
historical_bucket_impact: [uncertain] ApplyPilot's approved July 6 design reported 8.3% historical success for `other` ATS hosts and 44.7% of non-applied failures in agent/browser runtime, while the later fleet snapshot reached $1.8530 all-in per applied. Skyvern could address the variable-form slice of that runtime bucket but should not be used to spend more on already low-yield or policy-denied pages. Savings equal avoided premium-agent cost minus Skyvern, fallback, human, and maintenance cost; the necessary route-level counts and completion deltas have not yet been measured.
recommendation: CANARY, not broad adoption. Use Skyvern as the final model-assisted route for approved long-tail forms after deterministic adapters, HTTP preflight, and a semantic-locator layer fail. Start in shadow mode, keep ApplyPilot's answer provenance and exactly-once submit guard, require positive confirmation evidence, and promote per host only when the upper-bound quality risk is no worse and all-in cost per verified apply is below the current route. Do not use managed anti-bot or CAPTCHA capabilities to bypass access controls.
